text: The video shows a distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy with en bloc resection of the celiac axis. The surgical team uses the standard XI da Vinci robotic system for port placement. The procedure begins with an inspection for metastases and a laparoscopic ultrasound of the retroperitoneum. Dissection is performed from right to left to safely isolate the portal vein tumor interface, starting at the porta hepatis.

The surgical field shows significant fibrosis due to treatment effects. The surgeon identifies and mobilizes the replaced common hepatic artery. After incising the inferior border of the pancreas, the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) is identified, and a retropancreatic tunnel is created. The common hepatic artery is retracted laterally to expose the portal vein. The pancreas is transected with a vessel sealer up to the tumor involvement with the portal vein. The splenic vein is controlled with a vessel loop and transected with a stapler.

The surgeon isolates the area where the pancreas adheres to the portal vein. A bulldog clamp is placed across the portal vein, ensuring it is not significantly narrowed. The portal vein is sharply transected and removed en bloc. The vessel is then repaired by over-sewing with a 5-0 Prolene suture. Dissection continues in the retroperitoneum, removing nodes en bloc.

The surgeon retracts the stomach anteriorly to expose the celiac axis, which is dissected further. The pancreas is retracted anteriorly and to the left, revealing the area of celiac involvement. Dissection continues down the celiac artery to the level of the aorta, noting the proximal left gastric takeoff. An ICG angiogram is used to assist in identifying the main celiac trunk and the takeoffs of the left gastric and splenic arteries.
The surgeon transects the celiac axis with a stapler, taking it en bloc just distal to the left gastric artery. The remaining retroperitoneal attachments to the pancreas are mobilized.

The surgeon completes the gastric mobilization in both directions. The omental and splenic attachments are divided, freeing the specimen completely. The specimen is placed in an endocatch bag and removed from the abdomen. The pancreatic margin is sent for frozen section analysis. Due to treatment effects, the frozen section is indeterminate for cancer, prompting the surgeon to take an additional margin in two pieces. Additional retroperitoneal lymphadenectomy is performed, removing nodes from the aorto-caval groove.

The surgeon over-sews the cut edge of the pancreas with a running proline suture and applies a layer of Dermabond, which is polymerized to minimize post-operative leakage. The surgical field shows the sealed pancreatic margin.
